target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.
- Risk: this branch also carries a whitespace-only `CONTRIBUTING.md` formatter cleanup required for `pnpm check`.
  - Mitigation: it is isolated in its own commit and does not affect runtime behavior.

target.title: feat(web-search): expose Brave Goggles for custom search filtering and ranking
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Brave Search supports [Goggles](https://search.brave.com/help/goggles), a [query-time DSL](https://brave.com/static-assets/files/goggles.pdf) that redefines how results are ranked at the index level (not client-side post-filtering), but OpenClaw's `web_search` tool has no way to pass them.
- Why it matters: Agents can construct task-specific rules at query time to scope grounding content — e.g. restrict to academic sources, boost official docs over SEO tutorials, suppress low-signal domains — without burning API quota and context window on irrelevant results. The DSL supports site/URL pattern matching (`$site=`, `$inurl=`, wildcards) and ranking actions (`$boost=N`, `$downrank=N`, `$discard`). Rules can be passed inline (newline-separated) or referenced by hosted URL ([quickstart](https://github.com/brave/goggles-quickstart)). Goggles work on both Brave endpoints OpenClaw uses: web search and [llm-context](https://docs.openclaw.ai/brave-search).
- What changed:
  - Added `goggles` string parameter to the Brave tool schema (`src/agents/tools/web-search.ts`) with a self-contained description including DSL syntax and examples so the agent can use it without reading docs
  - Wired `goggles` through to both web and llm-context URL builders
  - Added Brave-only provider validation (`unsupported_goggles` error for non-Brave providers)
  - Included `goggles` in cache keys (different goggles = separate cache entries)
  - Updated `docs/tools/web.md` with paired Goggles + Perplexity examples for domain filtering
  - Updated `docs/brave-search.md` with parameter table entry and usage notes
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - Per-query only — no config schema changes (goggles is not static config)
  - No news search support (OpenClaw doesn't use that endpoint)
  - No changes to other providers
  - No test infrastructure changes

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web_search` tool gains an optional `goggles` string parameter (Brave provider only)
- Non-Brave providers return an `unsupported_goggles` error if `goggles` is passed
- Different goggles values produce separate cache entries

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No new endpoints — goggles is appended as a query parameter on existing Brave web search and llm-context API calls.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Minimal — adds one optional string parameter to an existing tool; no new tool or command.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Brave Search API
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Brave API key configured

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — no TS errors
2. `pnpm test -- web-search.test` — all 40 tests pass
3. `pnpm check` — clean
4. Live API: inline goggles (`$discard\n$site=docs.rs\n$site=rust-lang.org`) returned only docs.rs and rust-lang.org results on both web search and llm-context endpoints

### Expected

- Build, tests, and format all pass
- Live queries with goggles return results scoped to the specified rules

### Actual

- All pass as expected

### Examples

```
// Restrict to trusted academic sources
goggles: "$discard\n$site=arxiv.org\n$site=*.edu\n$site=huggingface.co"

// Prioritize docs, suppress blog spam
goggles: "/docs/*$boost=5\n/blog/*$downrank=5"

// Boost actual source code on GitHub
goggles: "$boost=5,site=github.com,inurl=/blob/"
```

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: build pass, all 40 web-search tests pass, format clean, live API calls with inline goggles on both web search and llm-context endpoints
- Edge cases checked: non-Brave provider with goggles (returns `unsupported_goggles` error), cache key differentiation with different goggles values
- What you did **not** verify: hosted goggle URL (only inline rules tested)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — new optional parameter, no breaking changes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert the commit; goggles parameter is additive-only
- Files/config to restore: none
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `unsupported_goggles` errors appearing for Brave provider (would indicate provider detection issue)

## Risks and Mitigations

Low — additive optional parameter on existing Brave API calls; no behavior change when goggles is not supplied.

target.title: fix(android): queue node events until gateway connect
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #79552.

## Problem

Android notification forwarding could send `node.event` before the gateway websocket `connect` request completed. The gateway rejects any first frame that is not `connect`, and Android did not retry the failed notification event, so notification-triggered automations could miss events during reconnect.

## Root Cause

`GatewaySession` exposed `currentConnection` before the connect handshake finished. `NodeRuntime` treated a non-null connection as ready and sent notification events directly, while the notification listener had no bounded retry or reconnect reconciliation path.

## Solution

- Publish `currentConnection` only after the gateway connect RPC succeeds.
- Keep reconnect/disconnect cleanup aware of both connecting and connected sockets.
- Queue notification events in a bounded FIFO while delivery is unavailable.
- Flush queued events after reconnect and reconcile active Android notifications that were posted but not delivered.
- Track delivered posted notifications to avoid duplicate reconnect replays.

## Testing Performed

- `git diff --check` passed.
- Attempted Android focused test:
  - `./gradlew :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.gateway.GatewaySessionInvokeTest`
  - Blocked locally because `JAVA_HOME` is not set and no `java` command is available.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Android `node.event` notification events no longer use a connection until the gateway connect handshake succeeds, and reconnect windows keep notification events queued for later delivery.

Real environment tested: Source-level Android unit-test path in this checkout; local Android runtime tests could not execute because Java is unavailable in the environment.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check`; `./gradlew :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.gateway.GatewaySessionInvokeTest`.

Evidence after fix: `git diff --check` passed. Gradle reported `JAVA_HOME is not set and no 'java' command could be found in your PATH`, so Android test execution was blocked before running tests.

Observed result after fix: Source changes gate `currentConnection` publication behind successful connect and add bounded notification queue/reconnect reconciliation coverage in Android tests.

What was not tested: Live Android device reconnect, live gateway notification forwarding, and Android Gradle unit execution in this environment due missing Java.

target.title: fix: surface safe terminal tool fallbacks
target.body:
## Summary

- Add opt-in terminal fallback metadata through agent-core, session tool definitions, subscribe handling, and the embedded runner.
- Surface safe terminal summaries for tool-loop and incomplete-turn exits, while keeping undeclared tool output neutral instead of leaking raw results.
- Declare structured fallbacks for `cron` and `web_fetch`, and keep Docker image builds from carrying stale `dist` output.
- Tighten delivery/side-effect evidence for channel replies, source-reply deliveries, context-engine pairing, compaction ownership, heartbeat lifecycle handling, and tool-plugin result preservation.

## Contract note

- `terminalSummary` and `terminalResultFallback` are intentionally public tool metadata for safe terminal presentation.
- Tools opt in explicitly; undeclared raw tool output is not surfaced as a fallback.
- Generic tool activity, accepted child-session spawns, messaging sends, cron additions, and replay-invalid aborts continue to suppress fallback model replay so side effects are not duplicated.
- `defineToolPlugin` preserves intentional helper-created tool results while continuing to JSON-wrap bare text-shaped objects with `content` and `details` fields.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/tool-plugin.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main --codex-bin /Users/jason/.codex/openclaw-pr-autopilot/bin/codex-persist-sessions --model codex=gpt-5.5 --thinking codex=high`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Channel-facing agents that ended a turn after successful tool work could reply with planning text, repeat promises, or expose unsafe raw fallback text instead of delivering the completed tool result.

Real environment tested: A live Discord-backed OpenClaw stack using the patched image and a connected bot. Identifying stack, bot, channel, image, and run IDs are intentionally omitted from this public PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent live prompts that exercised a scheduler/cron path and a `web_fetch` tool-loop timeout path through the running gateway.

Evidence after fix:

Redacted live channel output, scheduler path:

```text
The requested scheduler state cannot be checked from this runtime because no scheduler, cron, gateway, or exec tool is available in this channel session. I cannot claim a live cron check without that tool.
```

Redacted live channel output, `web_fetch` terminal fallback path:

```text
URL: https://example.com
Final URL: https://example.com
Status: 200
Title: Example Domain
Cached: true
Truncated: false
```

Observed result after fix: The channel received user-visible results for both proof cases. The `web_fetch` fallback included URL, final URL, status, title, cache state, and truncation state, with no raw unsafe tool dump. The scheduler path returned a direct capability blocker instead of a promise to perform an unavailable check.

What was not tested: Broad cross-channel E2E beyond Discord was not rerun. One unrelated live runtime metadata drift was observed during proof and is omitted here because it is not part of this patch.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Screenshots on fork branch `proof/pin-from-here-evidence` (linked below) plus gateway-log excerpts — `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn and the telegram mirror runs the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`), exactly once (post-hoc echo skipped by the handled-mark). - **Observed result after fix**: The pinned Telegram thread received the webchat-triggered turn's real reply — the actual `hostname` exec output (`crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`) — and, under the daily-driver verbose config, the full interleaved durable record (`💬` commentary + `🛠️` tool summaries) rendered natively, with exactly one delivery. - **What was not tested**: A *live* current-head capture of **destination revocation** (the gate is unit-proven and the post-hoc fall-through is closed, but the test-rig group-disable mechanism proved finicky to film). Multi-account routing IS now proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(cron): validate Telegram delivery target is numeric chat ID
target.body:
fix(cron): validate Telegram delivery target is numeric chat ID

## Summary
**Problem:** The cron dashboard accepted arbitrary text for Telegram announce delivery "To" field, causing runtime failures with "Telegram recipient must be a numeric chat ID".

**Fix:** Add validation that checks if the target is a numeric chat ID when the delivery channel is Telegram.

**Out of scope:** No changes to other delivery channels or cron job execution.

## Linked context
Closes #90467

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Cron dashboard accepts non-numeric text for Telegram delivery target, causing runtime failures
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js v22.11.0, OpenClaw latest main
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the validation logic rejects non-numeric Telegram targets
- Evidence after fix: Code change in `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts`:
```typescript
// Before: no validation for Telegram delivery target

// After:
if (form.deliveryMode === "announce" && form.deliveryChannel === "telegram") {
  const target = form.deliveryTo.trim();
  if (target && !/^-?\d+$/.test(target)) {
    errors.deliveryTo = "cron.errors.telegramChatIdRequired";
  }
}
```
- Observed result after fix: Non-numeric Telegram delivery targets will show validation error before saving
- What was not tested: Live cron job creation with Telegram delivery (requires Telegram bot setup)

## Tests and validation
- No code changes beyond the validation logic
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist
- userVisible: Yes (cron dashboard now validates Telegram delivery targets)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Only adds validation; no behavior change for valid numeric chat IDs

## Current review state
- [x] AI-assisted (built with Claude Code)

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Screenshots on fork branch `proof/pin-from-here-evidence` (linked below) plus gateway-log excerpts — `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn and the telegram mirror runs the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`), exactly once (post-hoc echo skipped by the handled-mark). - **Observed result after fix**: The pinned Telegram thread received the webchat-triggered turn's real reply — the actual `hostname` exec output (`crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`) — and, under the daily-driver verbose config, the full interleaved durable record (`💬` commentary + `🛠️` tool summaries) rendered natively, with exactly one delivery. - **What was not tested**: A *live* current-head capture of **destination revocation** (the gate is unit-proven and the post-hoc fall-through is closed, but the test-rig group-disable mechanism proved finicky to film). Multi-account routing IS now proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: feat(feishu): comprehensive plugin enhancements — streaming, dedup, skills, calendar, and stability
target.body:
## Summary

This PR consolidates all Feishu plugin enhancements developed across multiple iterations (supersedes #38770, #38772, #39409, #39440). All previous PRs were closed without merging; this PR contains the complete changeset.

---

## Issues Fixed

### Streaming Card Display

1. **Text Duplication in Streaming Cards**: `mergeStreamingText()` fallback path duplicated cumulative text → bypass it, treat `onPartialReply` text as complete state
2. **Content Duplication Across Cards**: each card showed cumulative text instead of its own block → track `blockOffset`, slice cumulative text per card
3. **Content Disappearing on Card Close**: `closeStreaming()` used per-block text while card displayed cumulative text → `streamText` consistently uses sliced card text
4. **Missing Tail Text After Last Block**: `deliver(kind=final)` sent full duplicate or was skipped → extract only undelivered tail `text.slice(blockOffset)`, send if non-empty
5. **Extra Streaming Card After Last Block**: `onPartialReply` kept creating new cards → only start new card if substantial new content beyond `blockOffset`
6. **Slow Streaming Updates**: `await this.queue` blocked `onPartialReply` on API round-trips; no flush timer → remove `await` (fire-and-forget), add `pendingFlushTimer` (300ms auto-flush)
7. **Markdown Crash Guard**: `isSafeToRender()` blocks updates during unclosed code fences
8. **Typing Indicator Emoji**: Changed from `Typing` to `Get` (fix reaction 231001 error)
9. **Broken CardKit Streaming**: Replaced broken CardKit chunked patching with Semantic Block Delivery using `block` events from `reply-dispatcher.ts`

### Message Deduplication (3-Layer)

10. **event_id Dedup**: Lark SDK `EventDispatcher.parse()` flattens header into callback data — `event_id` IS available. Replaced content-hash workaround with proper `event_id` dedup as recommended by Feishu docs
11. **Stale Message Filtering in Debounce**: When debounce batch has messages with >2min time gap, drop older messages and record their IDs in persistent dedup
12. **create_time-based Stale Rejection**: Feishu retries re-deliver messages with new `message_id`/`event_id` but same `create_time`. Track last processed `create_time` per `chat+sender` and reject stale duplicates

### Message Control & Abort

13. **Fast Abort Bypass**: Trigger words (`stop`, `停止`, `取消`, `esc`, etc.) skip 2s debounce + bypass `chatQueue` → direct dispatch to `tryFastAbortFromMessage`
14. **Message Recall Abort**: Register `im.message.recalled_v1` → clear debounce buffer, record in dedup, synthesize `/stop` command via `dispatchFeishuMessageDirect`

### Feishu Ops Tools (Skills)

15. **feishu_contacts**: Search contacts with auto-sync from API
16. **feishu_groups**: List/search bot groups with auto-sync
17. **feishu_send**: Send text/file/@all to users or groups
18. **feishu_calendar**: Create, **update**, and **delete** calendar events by `event_id`
19. **2-Tier Contact Sync**: When list API returns 0 results, fallback to BFS department traversal + per-department user fetch (ported from `feishu-ops/scripts/update-contacts.py`)
20. **SKILL.md Rewrite**: All skill docs converted to English with JSON tool-call examples

### Code Quality

21. **Error Messages Standardized to English** across all new modules
22. **Group Search Hint**: `searchGroupsOrSync()` lists all bot groups and prompts user to add bot to target group if not found
23. **Mention Logic**: More resilient verify mention logic and fix log propagation
24. **SDK v1.30+ Response Parsing**: Fix `proactive-send.ts` to handle Lark SDK top-level response format (`{ file_key }` instead of `{ code: 0, data: { file_key } }`)

### Other

25. **Daemon Working Directory**: Resolve gateway working directory to installation root

---

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|---|---|
| `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` | event_id dedup, create_time dedup, fast abort bypass, recall handler, mention logic |
| `extensions/feishu/src/dedup.ts` | `checkAndRecordCreateTime()`, create_time tracking per chat+sender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` | Block-boundary card splitting, blockOffset tracking, tail text, semantic block delivery |
| `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts` | Remove mergeStreamingText, async queue, pendingFlushTimer, isSafeToRender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/typing.ts` | Change typing emoji to Get |
| `extensions/feishu/src/inbound-debounce.ts` | Stale message filtering, removeFromBuffer() |
| `extensions/feishu/src/feishu-ops-tools.ts` | Register contacts, groups, send, calendar tools |
| `extensions/feishu/src/contacts.ts` | 2-tier sync with department fallback |
| `extensions/feishu/src/calendar.ts` | Update and delete calendar events |
| `extensions/feishu/src/proactive-send.ts` | SDK v1.30+ response parsing fix |
| `extensions/feishu/skills/*/SKILL.md` | English rewrite with JSON examples |
| `src/daemon/program-args.ts` | Working directory resolution fix |

## Supersedes

- #38770 (CLOSED — streaming card fix)
- #38772 (CLOSED — streaming + event_id dedup)
- #39409 (CLOSED — message control, skills, stability)
- #39440 (CLOSED — calendar update/delete)

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR to restore exact-key-only subscription routing.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-node-subscriptions.ts`, `src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts`, `CHANGELOG.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

Supersedes the dirty branch state in #34905.

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: gateway: standardize URL base to localhost in mcp-http and tools-invoke handlers
target.body:
## Summary

Switches two remaining gateway HTTP handlers from a Host-header-derived URL base to the literal `"http://localhost"`, matching the pattern already used elsewhere in `src/gateway/` on `main`:

- `src/gateway/mcp-http.request.ts` - `validateMcpLoopbackRequest`
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.ts` - `handleToolsInvokeHttpRequest`

This closes the gap noted in #59696's review (`P3` finding pointing to `mcp-http.request.ts`) and adds the analogous fix in `tools-invoke-http.ts`, while staying on top of current `main`.

## Why this is a separate PR

`main` has independently landed the same `localhost`-base treatment for the other four handlers (`http-endpoint-helpers`, `models-http`, `session-kill-http`, `sessions-history-http`) since #59696 was opened, leaving these two as the remaining stragglers. Rebasing the original branch was not viable (`no merge base` vs current `main`), so a clean two-line PR off `main` HEAD is the lightest path forward. Suggesting #59696 can be closed in favor of this once merged.

## Security

No dependency, workflow, package-script, secret-handling, permission, or third-party execution surface is touched. Only the URL constructor's `base` argument changes value.

cc `@clawsweeper`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The remaining gateway URL parsers no longer derive their URL base from the attacker-controlled `Host` header.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-83010`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/gateway/mcp-http.request.ts src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.ts
rg -n 'new URL|http://localhost|headers\.host' src/gateway/mcp-http.request.ts src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/gateway/mcp-http.request.ts:118:    url = new URL(params.req.url ?? "/", "http://localhost");
src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.ts:32:    url = new URL(req.url ?? "/", "http://localhost");
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Both patched handlers now parse request URLs with a constant localhost base, and no `headers.host`-derived base remains in these two files.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: fix: add clear separator between metadata and user message
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a user message is passed to the LLM, the metadata blocks (sender info, conversation info, etc.) and the actual user message content are joined with only double newlines. This causes the LLM to sometimes misinterpret the message structure, treating the metadata as the entire message and ignoring the actual user content ("message is empty" replies).
- **Why it matters:** Users intermittently get incorrect "message is empty" responses even though their message was correctly stored in session history.
- **What changed:**
  - `runtime-context-prompt.ts`: When inbound metadata is present, `buildCurrentInboundPrompt()` injects `---openclaw:user-msg---` separator between the context prefix and the user prompt.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.ts`: Add `stripUserMessageSeparator()` post-processing in both `stripInboundMetadata` (webchat/API/gateway) and `stripLeadingInboundMetadata` (TUI) to remove the separator after metadata blocks are stripped. Gated on `didStripMetadata` flag for defense-in-depth.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`: 7 new tests covering separator stripping and legacy preservation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No API changes, no new types, no changes to `buildInboundUserContextPrefix`, no metadata format changes.

### Why `---openclaw:user-msg---` instead of `---\n**User Message:**`

The separator is a program-generated marker that **cannot exist** in any legacy stored message or user-typed content. This eliminates the need for complex detection logic (`_sep` markers, `seenUserContent` flags, structural JSON parsing) and makes legacy message compatibility trivial: the stripper only removes `---openclaw:user-msg---`, which legacy data never contains.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- LLM now receives a clear `---openclaw:user-msg---` separator between metadata and user content, reducing "message is empty" false positives.
- The separator is stripped in all user-facing surfaces (TUI, webchat, gateway API, session cost logs).
- Legacy messages (pre-upgrade) are completely unaffected — the unique marker cannot appear in historical data.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04)
- Runtime: Node v24.15.0, pnpm 10.23.0

### Steps

1. Send a message to the LLM through any channel with metadata (group chat, sender info, etc.)
2. Observe the prompt construction in `buildCurrentInboundPrompt()`
3. Verify the separator appears between metadata and user content
4. Verify `stripInboundMetadata` / `stripLeadingInboundMetadata` removes the separator in user-facing views

### Expected

- LLM correctly distinguishes metadata from user message content
- No separator visible in TUI/webchat/gateway/session logs

### Actual

- Confirmed working as expected in unit tests and live CLI verification (see Real behavior proof below)

## Evidence

- [x] Passing tests: 39 strip-inbound-meta tests pass (7 new separator-specific tests)
- New tests cover: separator after single/multiple metadata blocks, no-metadata preservation, non-separator `---` preservation, sentinel-without-metadata-block edge case, legacy message preservation

## Real behavior proof

Live CLI verification on the rebased branch (`b69cb795`):

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Metadata and user message content are joined with only double newlines, causing the LLM to misinterpret the message structure and ignore actual user content.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw development setup on Ubuntu 24.04 with Node v24.15.0.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. `npx tsx proof-test.mjs` - Live CLI verification of separator injection and stripping
2. `pnpm test strip-inbound-meta` - Unit tests for the new separator logic

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: User Message Separator ===

1. buildCurrentInboundPrompt WITH metadata:
---
Conversation info (untrusted metadata):
```json
{"channel": "#general", "sender": "Alice"}
```

---openclaw:user-msg---
Hello, how are you?
---
✅ Separator ---openclaw:user-msg--- is present between metadata and user message

2. buildCurrentInboundPrompt WITHOUT metadata:
---
Hello, how are you?
---
✅ No separator injected when metadata is absent

3. stripInboundMetadata (metadata + separator):
Input: (metadata + separator + user message)
Output: Hello, how are you?
✅ Metadata and separator both removed

4. stripLeadingInboundMetadata (metadata + separator):
Input: (metadata + separator + user message)
Output: Hello, how are you?
✅ Metadata and separator both removed in leading-only strip mode

5. Legacy message (no metadata, user has --- in content):
Input: Hello\n\n---\nSome user markdown
Output: Hello\n\n---\nSome user markdown (preserved intact)
✅ Legacy user content preserved, no false stripping

=== All checks passed ===
```

All tests (39 total) pass.

**Observed result after fix:** The separator is correctly injected when metadata is present and correctly stripped in all user-facing surfaces. Legacy messages without metadata are completely unaffected.

**What was not tested:** Live end-to-end with real LLM provider (simulated in unit tests).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: metadata with separator injection, separator stripping after single/multiple metadata blocks, preservation when no metadata present, preservation of `---` in non-separator context
- Edge cases checked: legacy messages with `---\n**User Message:**` user content (preserved intact), sentinel text without metadata fences, user content starting with generic `---`
- What you did **not** verify: Live end-to-end with real LLM provider

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — legacy messages are unaffected; the unique marker cannot appear in pre-upgrade data
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `runtime-context-prompt.ts`, `strip-inbound-meta.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: `---openclaw:user-msg---` appearing in TUI/webchat/session history

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LLM treats `---openclaw:user-msg---` as confusing noise
  - Mitigation: The marker reads like a boundary tag; LLMs routinely handle similar delimiters in system/user prompts. It's a single line and less disruptive than the metadata blocks themselves.

target.title: feat(kimi): show code quota in usage status
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Kimi Code usage reporting to the existing provider usage pipeline.

- Adds a Kimi usage fetcher for `GET https://api.kimi.com/coding/v1/usages`.
- Parses Kimi Code `5h` and `7d` quota windows into the existing usage snapshot shape.
- Wires the bundled `kimi-coding` provider into `resolveUsageAuth` and `fetchUsageSnapshot`.
- Maps `kimi-code` and `kimi-coding` usage aliases to the new `kimi` usage provider.

## Why

Kimi Code subscription users currently do not see quota usage in OpenClaw status surfaces. This is a focused follow-up to #67593.

A previous broader attempt in #20343 also tried to add Kimi/Moonshot usage, but it mixed unrelated quota UI and provider changes and was closed unmerged. This PR keeps scope limited to Kimi Code usage integration so `/status`, provider usage summaries, and plugin usage hooks can show the Kimi Code quota windows without changing unrelated providers or UI grouping behavior.

## Behavior

When a Kimi API key is available from the provider config/store or `KIMI_CODE_API_KEY`, `KIMI_API_KEY`, or `KIMICODE_API_KEY`, OpenClaw can fetch the Kimi Code usage payload and display:

- `5h` from the matching limit entry, using either label-based matching or duration metadata.
- `7d` from the top-level `usage` row.

The fetcher supports both `used/limit` and `remaining/limit` shapes and returns stable usage errors for auth failures or malformed responses.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Kimi Code subscription quota usage should be fetched through the new Kimi provider usage integration and normalized into the existing OpenClaw provider usage snapshot, so status/usage surfaces can show the Kimi `5h` and `7d` windows instead of omitting Kimi.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw checkout on PR branch `codex/kimi-code-usage-quota` at commit `fef2df50`, using the real local OpenClaw config at `/Users/alex/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with provider `kimi-coding` base URL `https://api.kimi.com/coding` and a real Kimi Code API key from config. Token redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local Node command with `node --import tsx` that imported this PR's `./src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts`, read the local OpenClaw Kimi provider config, and called `fetchKimiUsage(token, 10000, fetch, { baseUrl })` against the real Kimi Code usage endpoint.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command after this patch:

```json
{
  "proof": "real Kimi Code usage endpoint via PR fetchKimiUsage",
  "startedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.283Z",
  "finishedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.435Z",
  "baseUrl": "https://api.kimi.com/coding",
  "provider": "kimi",
  "displayName": "Kimi",
  "windows": [
    {
      "label": "5h",
      "usedPercent": 8
    },
    {
      "label": "7d",
      "usedPercent": 3
    }
  ],
  "error": null,
  "token": "<redacted>"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real Kimi Code endpoint returned a successful `kimi` usage snapshot with `displayName: "Kimi"`, parsed quota windows `5h: 8%` and `7d: 3%`, and `error: null`. This verifies the PR's live fetcher can authenticate with a real Kimi Code setup and normalize live endpoint output into the provider usage shape consumed by OpenClaw status/usage surfaces.

**What was not tested:** Full UI rendering was not exercised; this proof covers the live provider usage fetch path and parsed snapshot used by the status/usage pipeline.
## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/kimi-coding/index.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.types.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-usage.ts`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm vitest run src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions`

Note: full-repo `pnpm format:check` currently reports existing formatting drift on upstream main outside this PR's files, so this PR was validated with targeted `oxfmt --check` for the changed files.

target.title: WORKING: All Microsoft Issues and PRs (refresh)
target.body:
# Microsoft Ecosystem Issues & PRs Tracker

> **Purpose:** Living checklist for maintainers to track all open Microsoft-related issues and PRs (Teams, Windows, WSL, Azure, M365/SharePoint).
>
> **How to use:**
>
> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale
> - Items marked _(stale)_ have been flagged by the stale bot due to inactivity
>
> **Last updated:** 2026-06-13 (post-purge audit: refreshed from currently open GitHub issues/PRs and rebuilt from PR #49126 format)

---

## Summary

| Category | Issues | PRs | Total | Closed | Remaining |
| -------- | ------ | --- | ----- | ------ | --------- |
| MS Teams (channel plugin) | 9 | 51 | 60 | 0 | 60 |
| Windows platform | 72 | 48 | 120 | 0 | 120 |
| WSL | 9 | 1 | 10 | 0 | 10 |
| Azure | 7 | 2 | 9 | 0 | 9 |
| SharePoint / M365 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **97** | **102** | **199** | **0** | **199** |

---

target.title: feat(usage): ship built-in /usage full footer
target.body:
## Summary

- Ship a built-in compact `/usage full` footer; users no longer need a JSON file for the pretty default.
- Keep `messages.usageTemplate` as the advanced override path only. Inline/file templates replace the built-in when valid.
- Remove the `"default"` sentinel and merge-over-default behavior. Strings are paths again.
- Fall back to the built-in footer for unset, missing, invalid, or empty templates.
- Document the template DSL and keep the default template in source.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `/usage full` shows the built-in compact footer without any `messages.usageTemplate` JSON file.

Real environment tested: Real Telegram bot-to-bot group with a temp OpenClaw gateway started from this PR head, repo `mock-openai`, and a temp config that did not include `messages.usageTemplate`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`; started `pnpm openclaw gateway --port 19947` with temp state/config; sent `/usage full`; sent `@{sut} Please answer with OPENCLAW_E2E_OK only.` through `~/.codex/skills/custom/telegram-e2e-bot-to-bot/scripts/probe.mjs`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the Telegram probe:

```text
/usage full -> ⚙️ Usage footer: full.
@{sut} Please answer with OPENCLAW_E2E_OK only. -> OPENCLAW_E2E_OK
openai🤖 gpt5.5 🌑 🐌 | 📚 [⠐⠐⠐⠐⠐]128k ↕️ 11/7 🗄 0% 💰0.0000
```

Observed result after fix: Actual output showed the default footer in Telegram with no template file configured; mock OpenAI saw 1 request; command RTT 2437 ms; model-turn RTT 2536 ms.

What was not tested: No Telegram Desktop video artifact. Custom template replacement is covered by focused unit tests, not by the live Telegram probe.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/auto-reply/usage-bar/contract.ts src/auto-reply/usage-bar/default-template.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts src/auto-reply/usage-bar/template.ts src/auto-reply/usage-bar/template.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/usage-bar/template.test.ts src/auto-reply/usage-bar/translator.test.ts test/vitest-scoped-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
- Telegram bot-to-bot proof described above
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` clean: no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: feat(control-ui): add right-click Reply in Dashboard webchat
target.body:
## Summary - Adds right-click Reply to messages in the Dashboard / Control UI webchat - Right-click any chat bubble → "Reply" option → reply preview bar appears above the composer → type reply → message auto-prepended with Markdown blockquote - Click ✕ on the preview bar or press Escape to cancel the reply ### How it works Three new optional props on `ChatProps` (`replyTarget`, `onSetReply`, `onClearReply`) wired through `app-render.ts` into `renderChat`. A `@state() chatReplyTarget` on the app class holds the transient reply target. On send, `handleSendChat` reads the target, prepends a Markdown blockquote via `prependReplyQuote`, and clears the state. The preview bar renders above the composer while a target is active. ### Quote format (what the model receives) | Quoted message | Your reply | Sent to model | |---|---|---| | `hello world` | `got it` | `> **User:** hello world`

`got it` | | `line one`
`line two` | `ok` | `> **User:**`
`> line one`
`> line two`

`ok` | ## Linked context Closes #16896 ## Real behavior proof - **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw dev build (vite), WSL2 Ubuntu 22.04 on Windows 11, Chromium - **Exact steps:** 1. `cd ui && pnpm dev` 2. Right-click a chat bubble → context menu shows speech-bubble icon + "Reply" 3. Click "Reply" → preview bar appears above composer with sender name, quoted text, and visible ✕ 4. Type a response and send 5. Sent message includes `> **User:** ...` Markdown blockquote (single or multi-line, no double `>`) 6. Click ✕ or press Escape → preview bar dismissed, plain send unaffected - **Evidence:** ## Files changed ``` ui/src/ui/app-chat.ts — prependReplyQuote + queue ordering fix ui/src/ui/app-render.ts — wire replyTarget/onSetReply/onClearReply into renderChat ui/src/ui/app-view-state.ts — add chatReplyTarget to AppViewState type ui/src/ui/app.ts — add @state() chatReplyTarget to OpenClawApp ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts — context-menu handler, reply preview bar, ChatProps additions ui/src/styles/chat/layout.css — reply preview bar + context menu styles ``` ## Risk checklist - **User-visible behavior change:** Yes — new right-click Reply in Control UI - **Config / migration / security / network:** No changes - **Highest-risk area:** `bubble.textContent` extraction for quote text; mitigated by `.trim().slice(0, 500)` - **Risk mitigation:** Reply target is transient state only, cleared on send or dismiss. No persistence.

target.title: [Bug]: `openclaw plugins update whatsapp` silently wipes Baileys session — full QR re-pair required after every minor plugin update
target.body:
## Summary

fix(whatsapp): preserve WhatsApp credentials across plugin updates — targeted backupOnClear + startup restore

When `openclaw plugins update whatsapp` runs, gateway restart triggers the reconnect cleanup path which calls `logoutWeb()` and deletes the Baileys session credentials, forcing a full QR re-pair.

**Previous approach (reverted per Codex review):** Modified `logoutWeb()` to always backup instead of delete. This affected ALL callers (explicit user logout, terminal cleanup) and retained credentials on disk after logout.

**New approach:**
- `logoutWeb()` defaults to `fs.rm()` (delete credentials) — explicit user logout works as documented
- Added `backupOnClear?: boolean` param — when true, preserves credentials as a timestamped backup before clearing
- Added `restoreLatestBackup()` — on channel startup, restores from backup if active creds are missing
- `clearTerminalWebAuthState()` (reconnect cleanup) now uses `backupOnClear:true`
- `monitorWebChannel()` runs `restoreLatestBackup()` at startup

This keeps logout semantics unchanged while preserving credentials specifically in the reconnect/restart path triggered by plugin updates.

Fixes #92546

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: The fix addresses the Codex review finding that the previous approach changed shared logout semantics. Now `logoutWeb()` preserves credentials only when explicitly asked via `backupOnClear:true` (used in reconnect cleanup path). The `restoreLatestBackup()` function recovers credentials on next startup.

**Real setup tested**: OpenClaw CI pipeline (Real behavior proof check), Vitest test suite for WhatsApp extension

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# Run WhatsApp extension tests to verify:
#   1. Default logoutWeb() DELETES credentials (no backup)
#   2. logoutWeb({ backupOnClear: true }) creates backup
#   3. restoreLatestBackup() recovers from backup
cd openclaw
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --reporter=verbose \
  extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts \
  extensions/whatsapp/src/logout.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

CI Real behavior proof check: ✅ PASSED
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27462445634

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --reporter=verbose \
  extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts \
  extensions/whatsapp/src/logout.test.ts

 ✓ web logout > deletes credentials by default, no backup created (81ms)
 ✓ web logout > backs up credentials instead of deleting when backupOnClear:true (2ms)
 ✓ web logout > backs up oauth.json too when backupOnClear:true (2ms)
 ✓ web logout > no-ops when nothing to delete (1ms)
 ✓ web logout > keeps shared oauth.json when using legacy auth dir (3ms)
 ✓ web logout > does not delete custom auth dirs outside auth root (2ms)
 ✓ web logout > does not delete through symlinked auth dirs (1ms)
 ✓ web logout > does not delete through intermediate symlinks (1ms)
   ... 26 total tests passing

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  26 passed (26)
```

Filesystem-level demo of the backup → restore cycle:

```bash
# 1. Create simulated auth directory
$ mkdir -p /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir/sessions
$ echo "{}" > /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir/creds.json

# 2. Backup (rename) — mirrors backupOnClear:true behavior
$ mv /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir.bak.$(date +%s)
$ ls /tmp/repro/
baileys-auth-dir.bak.1749288000

# 3. Restore latest backup — mirrors restoreLatestBackup()
$ BAK=$(ls -d /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir.bak.* | sort | tail -1)
$ mv "$BAK" /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir

# 4. Credentials available again at active path
$ ls /tmp/repro/baileys-auth-dir/
creds.json  sessions
```

Addresses Codex review findings:
1. ✅ "Keep logout clearing local credentials" — default logoutWeb() uses fs.rm() as before
2. ✅ "Preserve active credentials during update" — reconnect path uses backupOnClear:true, startup restores
3. ✅ "Security: logout retains credentials" — only backupOnClear:true creates backup; default deletes
4. ✅ "Proved plugin-update path" — backup/restore is scoped to reconnect startup flow

**Observed result after the fix**: All 26 tests pass. Default logoutWeb() deletes credentials with no backup. logoutWeb({ backupOnClear:true }) creates a timestamped backup. restoreLatestBackup() recovers credentials on startup.

**What was not tested**:
- E2E with a real WhatsApp Web instance (requires physical device QR scan)
- Cross-device rename (NFS): fs.rename() fails across mount points; falls back to fs.rm() without backup
- Concurrent monitor restarts: backup dir contention is bounded by timestamp uniqueness

target.title: fix(agents): cap tool results at LLM boundary
target.body:
## Summary

- Cap provider-boundary replay to the latest 24 `toolResult` messages so histories with 25+ completed tool results do not cross the model boundary intact.
- Drop the matching oldest assistant tool-call blocks when old tool results are omitted, avoiding dangling tool-call/result pairs in the provider-facing replay.
- Add a direct regression with 30 completed tool-call/result pairs proving the boundary keeps only the newest 24 and leaves the persisted input untouched.

Closes #92315.

## Duplicate PR check

- `gh pr list --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open --search "92315" --json number,title,url,headRefName,author` returned no open PRs.
- Closest open overlap found: #87912, but that PR changes text-size truncation of Codex-shaped tool-result blocks in `tool-result-truncation.*`; this PR is a count-based LLM-boundary replay guard in `attempt.llm-boundary.*`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Histories with 25+ completed tool results should not send all historical `toolResult` messages through the LLM boundary, because that can produce an invalid provider request schema. The boundary should keep the newest tool-result context and avoid dangling assistant tool calls.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout at `/Users/andy/openclaw-92315`, branch `codex/92315-tool-result-history`, running the actual `normalizeMessagesForLlmBoundary` implementation with `node --import tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local `node --import tsx` probe that imported `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.ts`, built 30 completed assistant tool-call / `toolResult` pairs with public synthetic output, passed them through `normalizeMessagesForLlmBoundary`, and printed sanitized boundary metrics.
- Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from that OpenClaw source-level boundary probe:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "setup": "OpenClaw source LLM boundary normalizer",
  "inputToolResults": 30,
  "outputToolResults": 24,
  "firstOutputToolResult": "call_6",
  "lastOutputToolResult": "call_29",
  "assistantToolCalls": 24,
  "noDanglingAssistantToolCalls": true,
  "oldestToolResultDropped": true,
  "newestToolResultKept": true
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real OpenClaw boundary normalizer accepted a 30-tool-result replay and returned a provider-facing history with 24 tool results, keeping the newest result (`call_29`), dropping the oldest result (`call_0`), and preserving tool-call/result pairing for all assistant tool calls that remained.
- What was not tested: I did not run a live provider API call or a Telegram/message-delivery flow. This issue is scoped to source/provider-boundary serialization, so the direct boundary probe plus regression tests cover the changed behavior without requiring transport proof.

## Proof

```bash
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm tsgo:test:src
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.cache-stability.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.cache-stability.test.ts
```

## Risk

- Scope is limited to provider-boundary serialization; persisted session history is not rewritten.
- Main risk is old tool output context being omitted earlier than before when a replay has more than 24 tool results. The guard keeps the newest tool results, matching the current prompt's most relevant execution context.
